queue.title: Building SignFlow: A full client-lifecycle CRM for service businesses
queue.angle: Technical case study — how we replaced 5 tools with one product, architectural decisions, and what we'd do differently

queue.title: Rebuilding Capital Property Group: From a broken template to a polished real estate platform
queue.angle: Before/after case study — the problems with their original site, the redesign decisions, and the business impact

queue.title: How DealProp's AI deal analyzer went from hack to production feature
queue.angle: The specific technical journey of building a production AI feature — prompt engineering, edge cases, hallucination handling, cost optimization

queue.title: Three patterns for multi-tenant SaaS, and when each one breaks
queue.angle: Compare schema-per-tenant, row-level-security, and app-level scoping. Pull examples from DealProp (RLS), SignFlow (row-level), Spite House (hybrid). Talk about what Stripe, Vercel, and Linear do. Opinion on which pattern wins for indie devs vs enterprise.

queue.title: The AI features users actually want (and the ones they don't)
queue.angle: A pattern we've seen across projects: AI features marketed as 'AI does X for you' fail; AI features marketed as 'AI gives you a head start on X' succeed. Examples from DealProp, other Cortex work, plus industry examples from GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Linear, Notion AI. The framing problem is the product problem.

queue.title: What custom SaaS actually costs in 2026
queue.angle: Real price ranges for different project sizes — $20k, $50k, $100k, $250k — what you get at each level, where the money goes, and what SHOULD be off-the-shelf. Reference industry benchmarks (Clutch, Upwork, typical US dev agency rates). Honest about where Cortex fits on that spectrum.
keywords: how much does custom software cost

queue.title: Why your SaaS bill tripled after launch (and how to fix it)
queue.angle: The hidden cost patterns: egress fees, per-request pricing, unoptimized database queries, background job queues. Reference patterns from multiple Cortex projects AND public postmortems from companies like 37signals leaving the cloud, Ahrefs on AWS costs, Cloudflare's pitch vs reality. Include a simple audit checklist.

queue.title: Serverless, containers, or self-hosted: our decision framework after 50+ projects
queue.angle: When to pick each, with real examples from Cortex projects at different tiers. Industry context: why Vercel works for marketing sites but fails for long-running jobs, why Railway is winning mid-sized SaaS, why self-hosted is making a comeback (Hetzner, Coolify). Include decision flowchart.

queue.title: We tried to stop using Next.js. Here's what happened.
queue.angle: Honest evaluation of Remix, TanStack Start, SvelteKit, Qwik, Astro — what each does better, what we actually tried on internal projects, why we came back to Next.js. Discuss where each alternative IS the right call. This isn't a Next.js love letter.

queue.title: How to choose a custom software developer in 2026
queue.angle: A genuinely helpful buyer's guide. Questions to ask, red flags, how to evaluate portfolios, pricing structures at different agencies, what contracts should include, ownership of code, what 'fixed price' really means. Almost no Cortex self-promotion — this is pure industry guidance.

queue.title: Five things nobody tells you about shipping AI features
queue.angle: Production realities of shipping LLM-powered features: prompt injection, cost blowouts, hallucination handling, latency, fallbacks when the model is down. Examples from DealProp's AI deal analyzer and public stories from companies like Notion AI, Intercom Fin, Linear. The gap between demos and production is a chasm.

queue.title: Why service businesses outgrow Jobber, HoneyBook, and ServiceTitan
queue.angle: The industry-specific patterns we see: what these tools do well, where they break, when a business should consider custom. Reference SignFlow as one of many paths, but mostly talk about the SaaS space itself. Pricing comparisons, feature gap analysis, switching costs.

queue.title: The real estate investor tech stack in 2026
queue.angle: Deep dive on tools real estate investors use: PropStream, DealCheck, REIPro, REsimpli, BRRRR calculators. What works, what doesn't, where DealProp fits. Honest about when you should use existing tools vs build custom.

queue.title: A real Core Web Vitals audit: from 8.2s LCP to 0.9s
queue.angle: Walk through a real client performance fix with actual numbers. The specific bottlenecks (unoptimized images, blocking JS, CSS-in-JS runtime, oversized fonts, too many hydration boundaries). The code changes that mattered. Generic advice vs what actually moved the needle.

queue.title: The OWASP Top 10 mistakes we see in small-business SaaS
queue.angle: Real security issues we've found in client code reviews: broken access control, injection, SSRF, insecure direct object references, weak auth, exposed admin endpoints. Specific code examples of wrong vs right. This is Cortex demonstrating expertise, not selling.

queue.title: From 8 spreadsheets to one dashboard: a small-business transformation
queue.angle: Tell a story of a client going from spreadsheet chaos to a custom internal tool. The process, the cost, the timeline, the ROI. Use composite details drawn from multiple real Cortex projects. Honest about what we got right and what took longer than expected.

queue.title: Why your business website is costing you customers (6 patterns we keep seeing)
queue.angle: The specific site issues that hurt conversion: slow mobile load times, hidden contact info, dead blogs, generic hero sections, broken forms, no social proof. Real examples from sites we've audited (without naming names). Industry data on conversion rates.

queue.title: Stripe, Lemon Squeezy, or Paddle: what to actually use
queue.angle: The real differences nobody talks about: international tax handling, chargeback rates, platform policies, API ergonomics, pricing math at different scales. What we use on DealProp, SignFlow, and Spite House (hint: different ones for different reasons). When a Merchant of Record beats rolling your own.

queue.title: Where no-code hits the wall (and when it's still the right call)
queue.angle: Honest look at Webflow, Bubble, Softr, Retool, n8n. What they're genuinely great for. Where they fall apart (performance, ownership, specific logic, scaling). Industry examples of companies that grew on no-code then had to rebuild. The Cortex take: we use n8n and Retool on our own ops, we don't build client products on them.

queue.title: The data center comeback: why companies are leaving AWS
queue.angle: Industry trend piece. 37signals' well-known exit, the economics of bare metal, Hetzner's momentum, the self-hosted SaaS movement. What this means for small dev shops and their clients. Not self-promotional — this is industry commentary.

style_guide.voice: First person plural (we/us/our), direct, confident, plain-spoken. Not marketing fluff.
style_guide.length: 1000-1500 words for most posts, 1500-2000 for deep case studies
required: Specific numbers and real examples, not vague generalities
Honest trade-offs — nothing is presented as a silver bullet
For case studies: reference the project by name with specific detail
For non-case-studies: pull examples from MULTIPLE Cortex projects where relevant AND freely reference industry patterns, other companies, tools, and trends
At least one opinion or contrarian take per post
Strong opening hook — no throat-clearing intros
forbidden: Marketing jargon (synergy, leverage, robust solutions, etc.)
Treating Cortex as the only studio that exists in the industry
Forcing every post to be about a single Cortex project
